1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

88	/-! ## §1. Line bundles on a product
89	
90	A *line bundle* on the relative curve `C ×_S T` is an invertible
91	`O_(C ×_S T)`-module, i.e. a sheaf of modules locally free of rank one. In
92	Mathlib `b80f227` the `Scheme.Modules` category is in place
93	(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`), but there is no `IsInvertible`
94	predicate on its objects yet (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for rings,
95	in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`). The iter-174 file-skeleton therefore
96	encodes the type of line bundles on the product by a typed `sorry` at the type
97	level; iter-175+ will instantiate it as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules`
98	cutting out invertibility.
99	
100	Blueprint reference: `def:line_bundle_on_product` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
101	functor"; Stacks tag 01HK). -/
102	
103	/-- The type of **line bundles on the product** `C ×_S T`: sheaves of
104	`O_(C ×_S T)`-modules, where the product is the categorical pullback of the
105	projections `πC : C ⟶ S` and `πT : T ⟶ S`. In Kleiman's notation with `X = C`
106	and base `S`, this is `Pic_C(T) = Pic(C ×_S T)`, the value of the absolute
107	Picard functor at `T`.
108	
109	iter-186: instantiated as `(Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules`, i.e. the
110	category of all `O`-modules on the product. This is a deliberate
111	simplification: Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `IsInvertible` predicate on
112	`Scheme.Modules`, so we forget the invertibility constraint at the carrier
113	level — the iso-class setoid + the quotient-set definition in §4 still
114	produces a meaningful set-valued presheaf. iter-187+: refine by pairing
115	the carrier with an invertibility witness (project-side predicate via
116	local trivialisation on an affine open cover, or upstream Mathlib once an
117	`IsInvertible`-on-`Scheme.Modules` predicate is added). -/
118	@[reducible] noncomputable def OnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (πC : C ⟶ S)
119	    (πT : T ⟶ S) : Type (u+1) :=
120	  (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules
121	
122	/-! ## §2. The pullback functor along the projection
123	
124	For a morphism of schemes `f : Y ⟶ Z` and an invertible `O_Z`-module `M`, the
125	pullback `f^* M` is an invertible `O_Y`-module (the pullback of an
126	open-cover-trivialisation of `M` trivialises `f^* M`, and the pullback of a free
127	module of rank one is free of rank one — Stacks tag 01HH). Specialising to the
128	projection `π_T = pullback.snd πC πT : C ×_S T ⟶ T` gives the pullback map
129	`π_T^* : Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_S T)`. Mathlib's underlying `Scheme.Modules.pullback`
130	functor (in `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`) supplies the pullback on
131	the full category of sheaves of modules; iter-175+ will restrict it to
132	invertible sheaves once the `OnProduct` carrier is unpacked.
133	
134	Blueprint reference: `def:pullback_along_projection` (Kleiman §2; Stacks tag
135	01HH). -/
136	
137	/-- **Pullback along the projection** `π_T : C ×_S T ⟶ T` of a relative
138	curve. Sends an `O_T`-module `N` to the `O_(C ×_S T)`-module
139	`π_T^* N`, induced by the `Scheme.Modules.pullback` functor on
140	`Scheme.Modules`.
141	
142	iter-186: with `OnProduct := (pullback πC πT).Modules`, the body is the
143	direct application of Mathlib's `Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)`
144	functor at `N`. The invertibility-preservation step (Stacks 01HH) is
145	deferred to iter-187+ once `OnProduct` carries an invertibility witness. -/
146	noncomputable def pullbackAlongProjection {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
147	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (N : T.Modules) : OnProduct πC πT :=
148	  (Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N
149	
150	/-! ## §3. Composition of pullbacks
151	
152	The composition law for line-bundle pullback (Stacks tag 01HG) says that for any
153	composable morphisms `f : X ⟶ Y, g : Y ⟶ Z` and any line bundle `M ∈ Pic(Z)`,
154	the natural isomorphism `(g ∘ f)^* M ≅ f^* g^* M` makes the pullback functor
155	into a (pseudo-)functor `Sch^op ⥤ Cat`.
156	
157	Specialised to the relative curve setting: given `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, the
158	base-change morphism `g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T` satisfies
159	`π_T ∘ g_C = g ∘ π_{T'}` (definition of the fiber product). Therefore on
160	line bundles `g_C^* ∘ π_T^* = π_{T'}^* ∘ g^*`, with the equality realised as the
161	canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors via the pseudo-functoriality
162	of `Scheme.Modules.pullback` (Mathlib `Modules.pullbackComp`).
163	
164	Blueprint reference: `lem:pullback_compose` (Kleiman §2; Stacks tag 01HG). -/
165	
166	/-- **Composition of line-bundle pullbacks** along the projection. For a base
167	scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, test objects `T, T'` over `S`
168	via `πT : T ⟶ S` and `πT' : T' ⟶ S`, and a morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`
169	(encoded by the hypothesis `πT' = g ≫ πT`), set
170	`g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T`
171	(the base-change morphism, given by `pullback.map`). On the underlying
172	`Scheme.Modules.pullback` functors the two routes through the canonical
173	naturality square give canonically isomorphic objects:
174	```
175	            Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)
176	  T.Modules  ─────────────────────────────────────────────►  (C ×_S T).Modules
177	       │                                                              │
178	       │ Scheme.Modules.pullback g            Scheme.Modules.pullback g_C
179	       ▼                                                              ▼
180	 T'.Modules  ─────────────────────────────────────────────►  (C ×_S T').Modules
181	            Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT')
182	```
183	i.e. for any `N : T.Modules`,
184	```
185	(Modules.pullback g_C).obj ((Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N)
186	  ≅
187	(Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT')).obj ((Modules.pullback g).obj N).
188	```
189	
190	This natural isomorphism is the substantive content of the
191	"line-bundle pullback functor is well-behaved under composition" fact
192	(Stacks tag 01HG). At the level of the `OnProduct` carrier (which is a typed
193	`sorry` in iter-174; see §1) it descends to equality of isomorphism classes
194	`[g_C^* π_T^* N] = [π_{T'}^* g^* N] ∈ OnProduct πC πT'`.
195	
196	iter-186: closed via the chain
197	1. `Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g_C (pullback.snd πC πT)` (functor iso applied at `N`)
198	2. `Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr` for the equation `g_C ≫ snd πC πT = snd πC πT' ≫ g`
199	   (`pullback.lift_snd` on the `pullback.map` lift).
200	3. `(Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (pullback.snd πC πT') g).symm` (applied at `N`). -/
201	theorem pullback_pullback_eq {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
202	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T)
203	    (hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) (N : T.Modules) :
204	    Nonempty
205	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback
206	            (Limits.pullback.map πC πT' πC πT (𝟙 C) g (𝟙 S)
207	              (by rw [Category.comp_id, Category.id_comp]) (by rw [Category.comp_id, hg]))).obj
208	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N) ≅
209	        (Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT')).obj
210	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj N)) := by
211	  set g_C : Limits.pullback πC πT' ⟶ Limits.pullback πC πT :=
212	    Limits.pullback.map πC πT' πC πT (𝟙 C) g (𝟙 S)
213	      (by rw [Category.comp_id, Category.id_comp]) (by rw [Category.comp_id, hg])
214	  have key : g_C ≫ Limits.pullback.snd πC πT = Limits.pullback.snd πC πT' ≫ g :=
215	    Limits.pullback.lift_snd _ _ _
216	  refine ⟨?_⟩
217	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g_C (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).app N ≪≫ ?_
218	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr key).app N ≪≫ ?_
219	  exact ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT') g).app N).symm
220	
221	end LineBundle

223	namespace RelPicPresheaf

225	/-! ## §4. The relative Picard quotient (set-valued)
226	
227	The relative Picard presheaf `Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`
228	quotients the line-bundle group of the product by the subgroup pulled back from
229	the base. As a quotient of abelian groups it is a quotient set; equivalently,
230	the equivalence relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` is captured by a
231	`Setoid` on the line-bundle type `OnProduct πC πT`. The quotient
232	`Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` is the underlying set of the relative
233	Picard presheaf at `T`.
234	
235	The "preimage_subgroup" name follows the Kleiman/Stacks convention: the
236	quotient is by the preimage `π_T^* Pic(T) ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T)`, treated as a
237	subgroup (image of a group homomorphism); the project-side Lean encoding
238	extracts the *Setoid* that the quotient construction takes.
239	
240	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined` (Kleiman §2,
241	Def. `df:Pfs`). -/
242	
243	/-- **Well-definedness of the relative Picard quotient as a `Setoid`.**
244	
245	The equivalence relation on `OnProduct πC πT` defining the relative Picard
246	presheaf
247	```
248	Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)
249	```
250	is the relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`. This relation is reflexive
251	(`L ⊗ L⁻¹ = O_(C ×_S T) = π_T^* O_T ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`), symmetric (the inverse of
252	a line bundle in `π_T^* Pic(T)` is in `π_T^* Pic(T)`), and transitive
253	(`L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` and `L' ⊗ L''⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` give
254	`L ⊗ L''⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`); hence is an equivalence relation, encoded by a
255	`Setoid` on `OnProduct πC πT`.
256	
257	The substantive content is the well-definedness of the quotient: the canonical
258	projection `OnProduct πC πT → Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` is the
259	set-theoretic underlying map of the relative Picard presheaf at `T`.
260	
261	iter-186: with `OnProduct := (pullback πC πT).Modules`, the carrier is the
262	modules category. We encode the **iso-class setoid** `L ~ L' ↔ Nonempty (L ≅ L')`.
263	This is a substantive simplification: it identifies isomorphic modules
264	(giving the underlying set of `Pic(C ×_S T)` once invertibility is imposed),
265	but does not yet quotient by the pullback subgroup `π_T^* Pic(T)`. The full
266	quotient requires a tensor-product structure on `Scheme.Modules` (Mathlib
267	ships `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` but no monoidal structure on
268	`Scheme.Modules` directly at `b80f227`) plus an inverse for invertibles;
269	iter-187+: refine to `L ~ L' ↔ ∃ N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ≅
270	pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available. -/
271	noncomputable def preimage_subgroup {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
272	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) :
273	    Setoid (LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT) where
274	  r L L' := Nonempty (L ≅ L')
275	  iseqv :=
276	    { refl := fun _ => ⟨Iso.refl _⟩
277	      symm := fun ⟨e⟩ => ⟨e.symm⟩
278	      trans := fun ⟨e₁⟩ ⟨e₂⟩ => ⟨e₁ ≪≫ e₂⟩ }
279	
280	/-! ## §5. Naturality in the test scheme — the relative Picard presheaf as a functor
281	
282	For a morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S` (encoded by the equality `πT' = g ≫ πT`),
283	the base-change `g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T` induces a map
284	`g_C^* : Pic(C ×_S T) → Pic(C ×_S T')` on line bundles, which (by
285	`lem:pullback_compose` = `pullback_pullback_eq` above) sends the subgroup
286	`π_T^* Pic(T)` into `π_{T'}^* Pic(T')`. Therefore it descends to a unique map
287	on quotients
288	```
289	g^♯ : Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) ⟶ Pic^♯_{C/S}(T'),    [L] ↦ [g_C^* L],
290	```
291	making `Pic^♯_{C/S} : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` a presheaf.
292	
293	For the iter-174 file-skeleton, we pin the **object-level** functorial action
294	(i.e. the induced map of quotient sets for a single test morphism `g`). The
295	full `Functor` packaging (with the identity / composition laws supplied as
296	`map_id`, `map_comp` fields) is iter-175+ work once `OnProduct` and the
297	`preimage_subgroup` carrier are unpacked.
298	
299	Blueprint reference: `thm:pullback_natural` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
300	functor" + Def. `df:Pfs`; Stacks tag 01HG). -/
301	
302	/-- **The relative Picard presheaf is functorial in the test scheme.**
303	
304	Object-level functorial action: for any morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, there
305	is a canonical map
306	```
307	g^♯ : Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) ⟶ Pic^♯_{C/S}(T')
308	```
309	of quotient sets, factoring `g_C^*` through `preimage_subgroup πC πT` and
310	`preimage_subgroup πC πT'`. On representatives, `g^♯ [L] = [g_C^* L]` where
311	`g_C := id_C ×_S g`. The factorisation through the quotient is well-defined
312	because `g_C^*` sends `π_T^* Pic(T) ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T)` into
313	`π_{T'}^* Pic(T') ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T')` (this is the commutative-square content of
314	`pullback_pullback_eq`).
315	
316	iter-186: with the iso-class setoid `preimage_subgroup`, `Quotient.lift`
317	on `L ↦ ⟦(Scheme.Modules.pullback g_C).obj L⟧` is well-defined because
318	functors preserve isomorphisms. The identity / composition laws giving the
319	full `Functor (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` packaging follow from `pullbackId` /
320	`pullbackComp` and are iter-187+ work (they currently aren't needed by any
321	downstream consumer). -/
322	noncomputable def functorial {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
323	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T) (hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) :
324	    Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT) → Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT') :=
325	  let g_C : Limits.pullback πC πT' ⟶ Limits.pullback πC πT :=
326	    Limits.pullback.map πC πT' πC πT (𝟙 C) g (𝟙 S)
327	      (by rw [Category.comp_id, Category.id_comp]) (by rw [Category.comp_id, hg])
328	  Quotient.lift
329	    (fun L : LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT =>
330	      Quotient.mk (preimage_subgroup πC πT')
331	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g_C).obj L))
332	    (fun _ _ ⟨e⟩ => Quotient.sound ⟨(Scheme.Modules.pullback g_C).mapIso e⟩)
333	
334	end RelPicPresheaf
335	
336	end Scheme
337	
338	end AlgebraicGeometry

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54

112	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
113	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
114	
115	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
116	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
117	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
118	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
119	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
120	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
121	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.
122	
123	\begin{definition}\leanok
124	[Pullback along the projection]
125	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
127	  
128	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
129	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
130	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
133	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
134	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
135	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
136	  %  [\dots]
137	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
138	  % sense: given any family of  \(S\)-schemes \(T_i\), we have
139	  %     \(\)\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).\(\)"
140	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
141	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
142	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
143	  Let \(C/k\) and \(T/k\) be as in
144	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\)
145	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along \(\pi_T\)} is the
146	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
147	  in this chapter)
148	  \[
149	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
150	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
151	  \]
152	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module
153	  \(\mathcal{N}\) to the isomorphism class of \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}\), an invertible
154	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
155	  defined because pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules respects isomorphism, and is
156	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
157	  \(\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C\) that we quotient by in the definition of
158	  the relative Picard presheaf.
159	\end{definition}

168	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
169	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
170	
171	\begin{lemma}\leanok
172	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
173	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
175	  
176	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
177	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
178	  % includes composition)
179	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
180	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
181	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
182	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
183	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
184	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
185	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
186	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
187	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
188	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
189	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
190	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
191	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
192	  canonical projections, and write
193	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
194	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
195	  pullback maps
196	  \[
197	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
198	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
199	  \]
200	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
201	  \[
202	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
203	  \]
204	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
205	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
206	\end{lemma}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
209	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
210	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
211	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
212	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
213	\begin{proof}
214	  
215	  
216	\leanok
217	  The square
218	  \[
219	    \begin{array}{ccc}
220	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
221	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
222	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
223	    \end{array}
224	  \]
225	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
226	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
227	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
228	  there is a canonical isomorphism
229	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
230	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
231	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
232	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
233	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
234	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
235	  set-valued maps stated above.
236	\end{proof}

238	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
239	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
240	
241	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
242	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
243	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
244	
245	\begin{theorem}\leanok
246	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
247	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
248	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
249	  
250	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
251	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
252	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
253	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
254	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
255	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
256	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
257	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
258	  % topologies  by
259	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
260	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
261	  %  \end{dfn}"
262	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
263	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
264	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
265	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
266	  \[
267	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
268	  \]
269	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
270	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
271	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
272	  a subgroup). Define
273	  \[
274	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
275	  \]
276	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
277	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
278	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
279	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
280	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
281	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
282	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
283	\end{theorem}

295	% refinement: `r L L' := exists N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L tensor L'inv iso
296	% pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available.

298	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
299	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
300	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
301	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
302	% not a translation of an external argument.
303	\begin{proof}
304	  
305	  
306	\leanok
307	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
308	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
309	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
310	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
311	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a

318	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
319	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
320	
321	\begin{theorem}\leanok
322	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
323	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
324	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
325	  
326	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
327	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
328	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
329	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
330	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
331	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
332	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
333	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
334	  %  [\dots]
335	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
336	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
337	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
338	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
339	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
340	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
341	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
342	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
343	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
344	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
345	  \[
346	    \begin{array}{ccc}
347	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
348	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
349	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
350	    \end{array}
351	  \]
352	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
353	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
354	  unique set map
355	  \[
356	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
357	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
358	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
359	  \]
360	  through the quotients of
361	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
362	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
363	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
364	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
365	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
366	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
370	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
371	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
372	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
373	\begin{proof}
374	  
375	  
376	\leanok
377	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
378	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
379	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
380	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
381	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
382	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
383	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
384	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
385	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
386	  universal property of the quotient set
387	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
388	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
389	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
390	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
391	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
392	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
393	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
394	\end{proof}

435	    of \cref{lem:pullback_compose} is the data; the identity and composition
436	    laws are the lemmas \texttt{id\_eq} and \texttt{comp\_eq} of the
437	    quotient-induced map.
438	\end{itemize}
439	
440	No new core axiom is required. The chapter is a definitional / structural
441	construction on top of the existing Mathlib invertible-module + pullback
442	infrastructure, plus the project's relative-spectrum chapter
443	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) for any concrete Lean unfolding that the
444	prover wishes to perform on an affine open of \(C \times_k T\).
445	
446	\section{Out of scope}
447	\label{sec:lbp_out_of_scope}
448	
449	This chapter packages \emph{only} the line-bundle pullback functor on a
450	relative curve and the well-definedness + functoriality of the set-valued
451	quotient \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The following downstream constructions live in
452	separate sibling chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
453	\begin{itemize}
454	  \item \textbf{Group structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)} (A.1.c) --- the
455	    quotient is here treated as set-valued; the abelian-group enhancement
456	    (and the \'etale-sheafification step that turns the presheaf into a
457	    representable functor) belongs to the planned chapter
458	    \texttt{Picard\_RelPicFunctor.tex}.
459	  \item \textbf{Comparison theorem $\Pic_{X/S} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{(X/S)\zar}
460	    \hookrightarrow \Pic_{(X/S)\et} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$}
461	    (Kleiman §2 Thm~\texttt{th:cmp}) --- out of scope here (\'etale-sheaf
462	    refinement; A.1.c).
463	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) as a scheme} --- A.1.c +
464	    A.2.* (Hilbert / Quot schemes); requires the flattening-stratification
465	    chapter \texttt{Picard\_FGA\_FlatteningStratification.tex} (planned, not
466	    yet on disk; do not forward-reference).
467	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and abelian-variety
468	    structure} --- A.3 (out of scope; depends on \'etale-sheaf and Hilbert
469	    machinery).
470	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property / Jacobian morphism} --- A.4 (out
471	    of scope; consumes the entire Route~A stack).
472	\end{itemize}
473	
474	\section{Internal-consistency check}
475	\label{sec:lbp_consistency_check}
476	
477	The five declaration blocks form a connected \(\uses\) chain rooted in
478	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}:
479	\begin{itemize}
480	  \item \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} is a root (no internal \(\uses\));
481	    its math content uses Mathlib's invertible-module predicate (not chapter
482	    dependency).
483	  \item \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} uses
484	    \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}.
485	  \item \cref{lem:pullback_compose} uses

487	  \item \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} uses
488	    \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection}.
489	  \item \cref{thm:pullback_natural} uses
490	    \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
491	    lem:pullback_compose, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}.
492	\end{itemize}

244	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in

246	lemma pseudofunctor_associativity :
247	    (pullbackComp f (g ≫ h)).inv ≫
248	      Functor.whiskerRight (pullbackComp g h).inv _ ≫ (Functor.associator _ _ _).hom ≫
249	        Functor.whiskerLeft _ (pullbackComp f g).hom ≫ (pullbackComp (f ≫ g) h).hom =
250	    eqToHom (by simp) := by
251	  let e₁ := pullbackComp f (g ≫ h)
252	  let e₂ := Functor.isoWhiskerRight (pullbackComp g h) (pullback f)
253	  let e₃ := Functor.isoWhiskerLeft (pullback h) (pullbackComp f g)
254	  let e₄ := pullbackComp (f ≫ g) h
255	  change e₁.inv ≫ e₂.inv ≫ (Functor.associator _ _ _).hom ≫ e₃.hom ≫ e₄.hom = _
256	  have : e₃.hom ≫ e₄.hom = (Functor.associator _ _ _).inv ≫ e₂.hom ≫ e₁.hom :=
257	    congr_arg Iso.hom (SheafOfModules.pullback_assoc.{u}
258	      h.toRingCatSheafHom g.toRingCatSheafHom f.toRingCatSheafHom)

268	  let e₂ := Functor.isoWhiskerRight (pullbackId Y) (pullback f)
269	  let e₃ := (pullback f).leftUnitor
270	  change e₁.inv ≫ e₂.hom ≫ e₃.hom = _
271	  have : e₁.hom = e₂.hom ≫ e₃.hom :=
272	    congr_arg Iso.hom (SheafOfModules.pullback_id_comp.{u} f.toRingCatSheafHom)

279	      Functor.whiskerLeft (pullback f) (pullbackId X).hom ≫ (Functor.rightUnitor _).hom =
280	        eqToHom (by simp) := by
281	  let e₁ := pullbackComp (𝟙 _) f
282	  let e₂ := Functor.isoWhiskerLeft (pullback f) (pullbackId _)
283	  let e₃ := (pullback f).rightUnitor
284	  change e₁.inv ≫ e₂.hom ≫ e₃.hom = _
285	  have : e₁.hom = e₂.hom ≫ e₃.hom :=

314	variable (f : X ⟶ Y) (g : Y ⟶ Z) [IsOpenImmersion f] [IsOpenImmersion g]

316	/-- Restriction of an `𝒪ₓ`-module along an open immersion.
317	This is isomorphic to the pullback functor (see `restrictFunctorIsoPullback`)
318	but has better defeqs. -/
319	def restrictFunctor : Y.Modules ⥤ X.Modules :=
320	  letI α : X.presheaf ⟶ f.opensFunctor.op ⋙ Y.presheaf := { app U := (f.appIso U.unop).inv }
321	  SheafOfModules.pushforward (F := f.opensFunctor)
322	    ⟨Functor.whiskerRight α (forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)⟩
323	
324	/-- The restriction of a module along an open immersion. -/
325	abbrev restrict (M : Y.Modules) (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] : X.Modules :=
326	  (restrictFunctor f).obj M
327	
328	@[simp] lemma restrict_obj (M : Y.Modules) (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] (U) :
329	    Γ(M.restrict f, U) = Γ(M, f ''ᵁ U) := rfl

331	@[simp] lemma restrict_map (M : Y.Modules) (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] {U V} (i : U ⟶ V) :
332	    (M.restrict f).presheaf.map i.op = M.presheaf.map (f.opensFunctor.map i).op := rfl
333	
334	/-- The restriction of a module along an open immersion. -/
335	def restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso : pushforward f ⋙ restrictFunctor f ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
336	  letI := CategoryTheory.Functor.isContinuous_comp.{u} f.opensFunctor (Opens.map f.base)
337	    (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (Opens.grothendieckTopology Y) (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
338	  (SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp _ _) ≪≫ pushforwardNatIso _ (NatIso.ofComponents
339	      (fun U ↦ eqToIso (f.preimage_image_eq U).symm) fun _ ↦ rfl) ≪≫
340	    SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr (by ext U x; exact
341	      congr($(f.appIso_inv_app_presheafMap U.unop) x)) ≪≫ SheafOfModules.pushforwardId _
342	
343	/-- Restriction is right adjoint to pushforward. -/
344	def restrictAdjunction : restrictFunctor f ⊣ pushforward f := by
345	  refine pushforwardPushforwardAdj (by exact f.isOpenEmbedding.isOpenMap.adjunction) _ _ ?_ ?_
346	  · ext U x; exact congr($((f.app_appIso_inv _).symm).hom x)
347	  · ext U x
348	    have : (f.appIso U.unop).inv ≫ f.app _ ≫
349	      X.presheaf.map (eqToHom (f.preimage_image_eq U.unop).symm).op = 𝟙 _ := by
350	      rw [Scheme.Hom.appIso_inv_app_assoc, ← Functor.map_comp, ← X.presheaf.map_id]; rfl
351	    exact congr($this x)
352	
353	instance : IsIso (restrictAdjunction f).counit :=
354	  inferInstanceAs (IsIso <| (restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso f).hom)
355	
356	instance : (restrictFunctor f).IsLeftAdjoint := (restrictAdjunction f).isLeftAdjoint
357	instance : (pushforward f).Full := (restrictAdjunction f).fullyFaithfulROfIsIsoCounit.full
358	instance : (pushforward f).Faithful := (restrictAdjunction f).fullyFaithfulROfIsIsoCounit.faithful

361	lemma restrictAdjunction_unit_app_app (M : Y.Modules) (U : Y.Opens) :
362	    ((restrictAdjunction f).unit.app M).app U =
363	      M.presheaf.map (homOfLE (f.image_preimage_le U)).op := rfl
364	
365	@[simp]
366	lemma restrictAdjunction_counit_app_app (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens) :
367	    ((restrictAdjunction f).counit.app M).app U =
368	      M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (f.preimage_image_eq U).symm).op := rfl
369	
370	/-- Restriction is naturally isomorphic to the inverse image. -/
371	def restrictFunctorIsoPullback : restrictFunctor f ≅ pullback f :=
372	  (restrictAdjunction f).leftAdjointUniq (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f)
373	
374	/-- Restriction along the identity is isomorphic to the identity. -/
375	def restrictFunctorId : restrictFunctor (𝟙 X) ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
376	  SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatIso _ (NatIso.ofComponents (fun _ ↦ eqToIso (by simp))) ≪≫
377	    SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr
378	      (by ext : 3; simp [← Functor.map_comp, SheafedSpace.sheaf]) ≪≫
379	    SheafOfModules.pushforwardId _

382	lemma restrictFunctorId_hom_app_app :
383	    (restrictFunctorId.hom.app M).app U =
384	      M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (show U = 𝟙 X ''ᵁ U by simp)).op := rfl

387	lemma restrictFunctorId_inv_app_app :
388	    (restrictFunctorId.inv.app M).app U =
389	      M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (show 𝟙 X ''ᵁ U = U by simp)).op := rfl
390	
391	/-- Restriction along the composition is isomorphic to the composition of restrictions. -/
392	def restrictFunctorComp : restrictFunctor (f ≫ g) ≅ restrictFunctor g ⋙ restrictFunctor f :=
393	  have : (f.opensFunctor ⋙ g.opensFunctor).IsContinuous
394	      (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (Opens.grothendieckTopology Z) :=
395	    Functor.isContinuous_comp _ _ _ (Opens.grothendieckTopology _) _
396	  SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatIso _ (NatIso.ofComponents fun _ ↦ eqToIso (by simp)) ≪≫
397	    SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr (by ext : 3; simp [← Functor.map_comp, SheafedSpace.sheaf]) ≪≫
398	    (SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp _ _).symm
399

401	lemma restrictFunctorComp_hom_app_app (M : Z.Modules) :
402	    ((restrictFunctorComp f g).hom.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (by simp)).op := rfl
403	
404	@[simp]
405	lemma restrictFunctorComp_inv_app_app (M : Z.Modules) :
406	    ((restrictFunctorComp f g).inv.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (by simp)).op := rfl
407	
408	/-- Restriction along equal morphisms are isomorphic. -/
409	def restrictFunctorCongr {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) [IsOpenImmersion f] [IsOpenImmersion g] :
410	    restrictFunctor f ≅ restrictFunctor g :=
411	  SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatIso _ (NatIso.ofComponents fun _ ↦ eqToIso (by simp [hf])) ≪≫
412	    SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr (by ext : 3; subst hf; simp)
413

415	lemma restrictFunctorCongr_hom_app_app {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) [IsOpenImmersion f]
416	    [IsOpenImmersion g] (M : Y.Modules) :
417	    ((restrictFunctorCongr hf).hom.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (by simp [hf])).op := rfl
418	
419	@[simp]

/-! ## §1. Line bundles on a product

A *line bundle* on the relative curve `C ×_S T` is an invertible
`O_(C ×_S T)`-module, i.e. a sheaf of modules locally free of rank one. In
Mathlib `b80f227` the `Scheme.Modules` category is in place
(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`), but there is no `IsInvertible`
predicate on its objects yet (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for rings,
in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`). The iter-174 file-skeleton therefore
encodes the type of line bundles on the product by a typed `sorry` at the type
level; iter-175+ will instantiate it as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules`
cutting out invertibility.

Blueprint reference: `def:line_bundle_on_product` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
functor"; Stacks tag 01HK). -/

/-- The type of **line bundles on the product** `C ×_S T`: sheaves of
`O_(C ×_S T)`-modules, where the product is the categorical pullback of the
projections `πC : C ⟶ S` and `πT : T ⟶ S`. In Kleiman's notation with `X = C`
and base `S`, this is `Pic_C(T) = Pic(C ×_S T)`, the value of the absolute
Picard functor at `T`.

iter-186: instantiated as `(Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules`, i.e. the
category of all `O`-modules on the product. This is a deliberate
simplification: Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `IsInvertible` predicate on
`Scheme.Modules`, so we forget the invertibility constraint at the carrier
level — the iso-class setoid + the quotient-set definition in §4 still
produces a meaningful set-valued presheaf. iter-187+: refine by pairing
the carrier with an invertibility witness (project-side predicate via
local trivialisation on an affine open cover, or upstream Mathlib once an
`IsInvertible`-on-`Scheme.Modules` predicate is added). -/
@[reducible] noncomputable def OnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (πC : C ⟶ S)
    (πT : T ⟶ S) : Type (u+1) :=
  (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules
input.new_string:
/-! ## §1. Line bundles on a product

A *line bundle* on the relative curve `C ×_S T` is an invertible
`O_(C ×_S T)`-module, i.e. a sheaf of modules locally free of rank one. In
Mathlib `b80f227` the `Scheme.Modules` category is in place
(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`), but there is no `IsInvertible`
predicate on its objects yet (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for rings,
in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`). iter-187 supplies the missing
predicate as a project-side definition via local trivialisation on an affine
open cover (see `IsLocallyTrivial` below), and refines `OnProduct` to the
corresponding subtype.

Blueprint reference: `def:line_bundle_on_product` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
functor"; Stacks tag 01HK). -/

/-- A sheaf of `𝒪_X`-modules `M` is **locally trivial of rank one** if every
point `x : X` has an affine open neighbourhood `U` on which the restriction
`M|_U` is isomorphic to the structure sheaf-as-module `𝒪_U`. This is the
project-side stand-in for the missing Mathlib `IsInvertible` predicate on
`Scheme.Modules`: line bundles (invertible sheaves) are exactly the
`IsLocallyTrivial` modules, by Stacks tag 01HK / Hartshorne II §6. Local
trivialisability is the structural content that survives base change along
`f : Y ⟶ X` (Stacks 01HH) and is the source of the invertibility-preservation
step for `pullbackAlongProjection`.

The witness is bundled as `Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit _)`:
the existence of a sheaf-of-modules isomorphism over the affine chart. -/
def IsLocallyTrivial {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : Prop :=
  ∀ x : X, ∃ U : X.Opens, x ∈ U ∧ IsAffineOpen U ∧
    Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf)

/-- The type of **line bundles on the product** `C ×_S T`: invertible sheaves of
`O_(C ×_S T)`-modules, where the product is the categorical pullback of the
projections `πC : C ⟶ S` and `πT : T ⟶ S`. In Kleiman's notation with `X = C`
and base `S`, this is `Pic_C(T) = Pic(C ×_S T)`, the value of the absolute
Picard functor at `T`.

iter-187: refined to the subtype of `(Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules` cut out
by the project-side predicate `IsLocallyTrivial` (locally trivial of rank one).
The carrier is now genuinely the line-bundle subobject of `Scheme.Modules`,
matching the blueprint statement `def:line_bundle_on_product`. Iter-186's
all-modules carrier is recovered by forgetting the second component. -/
def OnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) : Type (u+1) :=
  { M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M }

/-- Projection from a line bundle on the product to its underlying
sheaf of modules. -/
abbrev OnProduct.carrier {S C T : Scheme.{u}} {πC : C ⟶ S} {πT : T ⟶ S}
    (L : OnProduct πC πT) : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules := L.1

/-- A line bundle on the product is locally trivial of rank one. -/
lemma OnProduct.isLocallyTrivial {S C T : Scheme.{u}} {πC : C ⟶ S} {πT : T ⟶ S}
    (L : OnProduct πC πT) : IsLocallyTrivial L.carrier := L.2

15	
16	Let `S` (resp.`R`) be a sheaf of rings on a category `C` (resp. `D`)
17	equipped with a Grothendieck topology `J` (resp. `K`).

22	in the category `SheafOfModules S` which corresponds to `φ`, and
23	show that the adjoint morphism
24	`pullbackObjUnitToUnit φ : (pullback.{u} φ).obj (unit S) ⟶ unit R`
25	is an isomorphism when `F` is a final functor.
26	More generally, the functor `pullback φ` sends the free sheaf
27	of modules `free I` to `free I`, see `pullbackObjFreeIso` and
